DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-20-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: At the first calling of the case counsel for Plaintiff was not present.
10:17am The matter is recalled. Both sides are present.

Appearances (by Zoom):
Raphael Metzger, Esq. 562-437-4499 Metzger Law Group for Plaintiff.
Anatolii Trembach, Esq. 213-443-5100 Wilson Elser Moskowitz Edelman & Decker LLP for Defendant LOWE'S HOME CENTERS, LLC.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

1 - DEFENDANT LOWE'S HOME CENTERS, LLC DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Defendant Lowe's Home Centers' demurrer to all six causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiffs Uriel Lopez and Pamela Rodriguez is overruled as to all six causes of action. All six causes of action are adequately alleged and are not uncertain. Lowe's request for judicial notice is denied. It is a longstanding and well-established rule that trial court opinions lack any precedential value and are not citable as legal authority in California courts.

Plaintiffs sue Lowe's for selling silica-containing stone products and alleges that Lowe's knew that there are toxic hazards associated with those products.

Lowe's argues that plaintiffs did not allege a duty supporting their first cause of action for negligence because "according to the Complaint, Lowe's was not involved in the chain of commerce." The complaint alleges otherwise.

The second and third causes of action for products liability sufficiently identify a defective product.

The fourth cause of action for fraudulent concealment adequately alleges a duty to warn. (Jones v. ConocoPhillips Co. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1187, 1200.)

The fifth cause of action for breach of implied warranties adequately alleges that the stone products were defective and that Lowe's impliedly warranted that they would be fit for their intended uses. Furthermore, Jones v. ConocoPhillips Co. controls here as well. There, the Court of Appeal held that a breach of implied warranty cause of action "is viable as to potentially hazardous chemical products manufactured and sold to companies for use by their own employees in the manufacturing process." (Id. at 1201-1202).

The sixth cause of action for loss of consortium adequately alleges causation.

A demurrer based on asserted uncertainty is disfavored and should only be granted when the complaint is so incomprehensible that it is impossible to respond. (Lickiss v. Financial Indus. Regulatory Auth. (2012) 208 Cal.App.5th 1125, 1135.) Plaintiffs' complaint easily passes this low bar.

An Order is signed for both motions. (see scanned order for details)

Visiting Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/HEK)

Defendant Lowe's Home Centers' motion to strike punitive damages allegations and specified portions of the complaint filed by plaintiffs Uriel Lopez and Pamela Rodriguez is denied in its entirety. Plaintiffs adequately allege ultimate facts showing conscious disregard and despicable conduct malice and a duty to warn for fraudulent concealment which sufficiently support their prayer for punitive damages. (Nissan Motor Acceptance Cases (2021) 63 Cal.App.5th 793, 829 ["Of course, fraudulent concealment is an intentional tort that may support a punitive damage award"].) Lowe's has not shown that any portions of the complaint should be stricken. Lowe's request for judicial notice is denied. It is a longstanding and well-established rule that trial court opinions lack any precedential value and are not citable as legal authority in California courts.
